nameMeanings.name: Waterton
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of Family Names in Britain and Ireland © University of the West of England 2016

nameMeanings.name: Sir
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: German: variant of   Sier  , unexplained. This surname is very rare in Germany.
Czech (Šír): nickname from Middle High German schīr ‘sheer, pure, clean’. Compare Schier .

variants.name: Sier
meanings: Dutch and German: from a short form of the personal name Sierik, Sierich, composed of the ancient Germanic elements sigi ‘victory’ + rīc ‘power(ful)’, or from a short form of the personal name Sigihard, composed of the ancient Germanic elements sigi + hari ‘army’.

nameMeanings.name: Knight
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: English: status or occupational name from Middle English knight ‘retainer, attendant’ (Old English cniht ‘boy, youth, lad)’. The specialized feudal sense ‘a high-ranking tenant bound to serve his lord as a mounted soldier’ is not known to have ever given rise to the surname, although it is not out of the question that it may occasionally have been used as a nickname, perhaps for someone who played the part of an armed knight in a local pageant.
Irish: sometimes adopted for Gaelic Mac an Ridire ‘son of the knight’. See also McKnight .

variants.name: Knights
meanings: English (East Anglia): variant of Knight , with post-medieval excrescent -s.

variants.name: McKnight
meanings: Irish: part translation of Gaelic Mac an Ridire ‘son of the horseman (Gaelic ridire)’, formerly Anglicized McRuddery.
Scottish (Dumfriesshire and Galloway) and Irish (Ulster): Anglicized form of Gaelic Mac Neachtain, which is usually Anglicized as McNaughton .

nameMeanings.name: Robert
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: One of the many French names of Germanic origin that were introduced into Britain by the Normans; it has since remained in continuous use. It is derived from the nearly synonymous elements hrōd ‘fame’ + berht ‘bright, famous’, and had a native Old English predecessor of similar form (Hreodbeorht), which was supplanted by the Norman name. Two dukes of Normandy in the 11th century bore the name: the father of William the Conqueror (sometimes identified with the legendary Robert the Devil), and his eldest son. It was borne also by three kings of Scotland, notably Robert the Bruce ( 1274–1329 ), who freed Scotland from English domination. The altered short form Bob is very common, but Hob and Dob, which were common in the Middle Ages and gave rise to surnames, are extinct. See also Rupert .

en.lifeSummary: Robert Waterton Sir Knight was born in 1500, in Walton, Yorkshire, England, United Kingdom. He had at least 1 son. He died in 1540, in Wakefield, Yorkshire, England, United Kingdom, at the age of 40.

fr.lifeSummary: Robert Waterton Sir Knight est né en 1500, à Walton, Everwyck, Angleterre, Royaume-Uni. Il a eu au moins 1 fils. Il est décédé en 1540, à Wakefield, Everwyck, Angleterre, Royaume-Uni, à l’âge de 40 ans.

de.lifeSummary: Robert Waterton Sir Knight wurde 1500 in Walton, Yorkshire, England, United Kingdom geboren. Er hatte mindestens 1 Sohn. Er starb 1540 im Alter von 40 Jahren in Wakefield, Yorkshire, England, United Kingdom.

it.lifeSummary: Robert Waterton Sir Knight è nato il nel 1500 nel Walton, Yorkshire, Inghilterra, Regno Unito. Lui avuto almeno 1 figlio. Morì nel 1540, nel Wakefield, Yorkshire, Inghilterra, Regno Unito, all'età di 40 anni.

ko.lifeSummary: Robert Waterton Sir Knight(은)는 1500년 영국 잉글랜드 요르크실르 월턴에서 태어났다. 그는 적어도 한 명의 아들을 두었다. 그는 1540년 영국 잉글랜드 요르크실르 웨이크필드에서 40세의 나이로 사망했다.
